California
Fair POlitical
Practices Comrnission
April 23, 199 O
Marilyn Wiczynski
Acting Deputy city Attorney
City of Garden Grove
P. O. Box 3070
carden Grove, CA 92642
Re:
Dear Ms. Wiczynski:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
concernj"ng the duties of two ci,ty councilmembers for the City of
carden crove under the conflict-of-interest 
provj-sions of the
Political 
Ref orn Act (the rrActrr) . 1
OUESTIONS
1. Does the ownership of his residence, which is located on
the street to be improved and which is between 80 and 920 feet
from the properties to be acguired (possibLy through
condemnations) and used for a street improvement project (the
project) by the City of Garden Grove , disqualify Councilmember
wittians from votinq on any or all of the condemnations or
acquis itions?
2. 
Does the ownership of his residence, which is
respectively between 370 and 560 feet from the proPerties to be
acgui,red (possibly through condemnati,ons ) and used for a street
inrprovernent project (the project) by the city of Garden Grove,
disqualify Councilnember Kessler from votinq on any or a1l- of the
condemnations or acquisitions?
1 Government code sections 81000-9101-5. A11 statutory references
Ere to the Government Code unl,ess otherwise indicated- 
Commission
regrulations appear at 2 caLifornia Code of Regulations. Section
18OOO, et seq. A11 references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California code of Regulations.
Your Request f or Adv]-ce
our File No. A-90-162
428 J Street, Suite 8OO o P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 958O4-O8O7 o (916)322-5660


our FiIe No. A-90-152
Page 2
coNcLUSl0NS
1. The ownership of his residence, which is on the street to
be improved and which is between 80 and 920 feet from the
properties to be acquired by the City of carden Grove (possibly
through condemnations) and used for a street improvement project,
would disqualify Councilnenber Williaros from voting on the
condemnations or acquisitions if the decision will have any
financial effect upon his property interest unless (1) all the
properties that are roughly the same distance away from the
proposed project are similarly affected and the property owners
constitute a significant segrment of the population or (2) the
effect on the reroainder of the population v/ilI be substantially
the same as the effect on him.
2. The ownership of his residence, which is respectively
between 370 and 560 feet from the properties to be acquired by the
city of Garden crove (possibly through condemnations) and used for
a street j.mprovenent project, would disqualify CounciLoernber
Kessler froro voting on the condemnatj.ons or acquisitions if those
decisions wou.l-d affect the value of his property by $10,o0o or
more or affect the rental- value of his property by $1,200 per year
or more unless (1) all the properties that are roughly the saroe
distance away from the proposed project are similarly affected and
the property owners constitute a significant segnment of the
population, or (2) the effect on the remainder of the population
will be substantj.ally the same as the effect on him.
FACTS
Project No. 735 encompasses chapman Avenue between Euclid
street and Ninth street, a distance of .6 rniles, all within the
city of carden crove. chapman Avenue is a main arterial street
within the City of Garden Grove. Chapman Avenue runs east and
west and is being widened from the existing width of 80 feet to
1oo feet to accoEmodate more traffic.
The project was approved for funding from the orange county
Transportltion comnission in the fiscal year 1988-89. It includes
ne!, paving, street wj.dening, and street lights.
A vote was taken on the awarding of a contract involving
Project No. 735 at the council neetinq of February 20, t99o.
Two counci lrnenbers, Frank Kessler and J. Tilnan Wi1lians,
abstained from votinq on the contract award because of the
location of their individual residences.
The project calls for the acquisitions of 20 feet of frontage
from 12 parcels on chapnan Avenue. This will take place by
condemnation or acquisition j.n the future and will need council
approval .
The Garden Grove city Council consists of four councilmembers
and a mayor, elected at Iarge.


Our Fi■e No. A-90-■62
Page 3
Councilmerober J. Tilman wi11iaEs, who lives at 11241 chapman
Avenue, which is a corner 1ot. The property is between 80 and 920
feet from the properties to be acquired.
Councilmember Frank Kessler lives on a private road at 11352
Bixler Drive. It has no direct access to Chapman Avenue. The
properties fronting Chapnan Avenue which will be affected by the
street widening project are between 37o feet and 560 feet from
Councilmember Kessler's hone. Councilmember Kessler's residence
is 285 feet from the north curb of chapman Avenue at its nearest
point.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any pubLic official 
from naking,
participating in naking, or otherl,rise usinq his or her official
losition to inftuence a governmentaf decision in which the of-
tici.f 
has a financial interest. 
city councilnembers are public
officials. 
(Section 82048 . )
Section 87103 specifies that a public official 
has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision wiII have a material financial effect,
distingnrishable from the effect on the public generally, on the
offici;I 
or a nember of his or her innediate farnily or on:
(b) Any real property in which the public of-
ficial 
has a direct or indirect interest worth one
thousand dollars (91,0oo) or more.
Section 87103 (b) .
Accordingr to the inforuation you have Provided, each of the
two councilmen owns a residence on one parcel of land. It is
assumed for purposes of this analysis that the value of each
property is greiter than 91,ooo and that neither is a source of
rental income.
Section 87■03 specifies that
financia■ interest in a decis■
on
ab■e that the dec■siOn w■■■ have
his property or source of income.
a public official 
has a
only if it is reasonablv foresee-
a material financial effect on
whether the financial consequences of a decision are reason-
ably foreseeable at the tiute a governmental decision is made
dep6nds on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur. certainty is not required.
Ho$rever, if an effect i,s only a nere possibility, 
it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (fn re Thorner (1975) 1 f'PPc ops. 198,
copy enclosed. )


Our File No. A-90-162
Paqe 4
Materialitv of the Financial Effect of the Decision on
Councilrnernber Williams' Interest
The materiality of the financial effect of the decision must
also be determined. Since the decision to acquire the property
for road improvement purposes, subdivision (a) (2) of Regulation
fa7o2.3 (copy enclosed) would apply to deterBine whether the
effect of the decision is material. That subdivision provides
that the effect of the decision is naterial if:
The decision involves construction of, or
inprovements to, streets, water, se$/er, storB
drainage or sinilar facilities, 
and the real
property in which the official 
has an interest will
receive nev, or substantially iroproved services.
Regulation 1a702.3 (a) (2).
Even though the decision does not specifically call for
inprovernent of roads adjacent to Councilmenber Williams' Property,
thL purpose of the acquisition is clearly for the improvement of
Chapnan Avenue. Improvement of that road, which at one point runs
adjicent to his property, will result j.n substantially iroproved
service to his property.
The fact that Councilmember Williaurs' property is less than
3OO feet from the project area also gj-ves rise to a somewhat
different analysis. If the property in which the official 
has an
interest is within 3oo feet of the property which j-s the subject
of the decj.sion, the effect on the officj-al's 
economic interest is
considered material rrunless the decision will have no effect upon
the official's 
real property interest." 
(Regxrlation
L87o2.3 (a) (1).) 
It would seeu that improveroents to chapman Avenue
would affect the value of Councilneuber Williams' property.
In light of the foregoing, it appears that councilmember
Willians nust disqualify hiurself from participation in the subject
decision unless (1) all the properties that are roughly the saue
distance away from the proposed project are sirnilarly affected and
the property owners constitute a significant seguent of the
population or (2) the effect on the remainder of the population
will be substantia]ly the same as the effect on him (discussed
below under the heading, 'rPubLic Generallyrr).
I'{ateriatitv of the Effect of the Decision on councilmember
KessLer's fnterest
The residence of Councilmember Kessler is within 37o feet of
the project area at its nearest point. 
Subdivision (a) of
Regulation la7o2.3 provides guidelines as to whether the effect of
a decision on the real property interest of a public official
where the property is outside a radius of 300 feet, yet within a
radius of 2,500 feet, is material. In these circumstances, the


Our File No. A-90-162
Page 5
effect of the decisi-on is materiar if the decisi-on will have a
reasonabLy foreseeable effect of:
(A) Ten thousand dollars (g1O,OOo) or
more on the fair market value of the real
property in which the official 
has an inter_
esti or
(B) Will affect the rental value of the
property by $1,000 or more per 12 nonth
period.
RegruLation La702.3 (a) (3) (copy
enclosed).
Thus, Councilroernber Kessler rnust disqualify hinself from
participating in any decision concerning the project that could
foreseeably increase or decrease the fair market-va1ue of his
property by $10,000 or more, or the rental value of his property
by at least S1,O00 in a 12-month period (Re$tlation
78702.3 (a) (3) (A)) unlesq (1) al1 the properties that are roughly
the saue distance away from the proposed project .are sinilari.y -
affected and tle property olrners constitute a significant segnnent
of the population or (2) the effect on the reroainder of the
population will be substantially the same as the effect on him
(discussed below under the heading, rtpublic Generallyrr).
We cannot deteraine the uragnitude of the financial effect on
Councikoember KessLerrs property that will be caused by the
decisions on the project. we must Leave this factuar dlternination
of nateriality to you within the guidelj.nes provided by Regulation
18702.3. Horrever, Regmlation lA702.3 (d) does set forth faitors
that you must consider in deteminingr whether the decisions will
have a material financial- effect on the value of an official,s
real property.2 You nust consid.er the foIlolring:
1. The proxinity of the property which is the
subject of the decision and the rnagnilude of the
proposed project.or change in use in relationship
to the property in which the official 
has an intLr-
est ;
2. Whether it is reasonabl-y foreseeable that
the decision will affect the developrnent potential
or incorue producing potential of the prop-rty;
3. In additj.on to the foregoing, in the case
of residential property, whether it is reasonably

specified in Reg"ulation 18702.3 are not lirnited to the factors
(d).

from the properties to be acguired (possibLy through

1. The ownership of his residence, which is on the street to
be improved and which is between 80 and 920 feet from the
properties to be acquired by the City of carden Grove (possibly
through condemnations) and used for a street improvement project,
would disqualify Councilnenber Williaros from voting on the
condemnations or acquisitions if the decision will have any
financial effect upon his property interest unless (1) all the
properties that are roughly the same distance away from the
proposed project are similarly affected and the property owners
constitute a significant segrment of the population or (2) the
effect on the reroainder of the population v/ilI be substantially
the same as the effect on him.
2. The ownership of his residence, which is respectively
between 370 and 560 feet from the properties to be acquired by the
city of Garden crove (possibly through condemnations) and used for

decisions wou.l-d affect the value of his property by $10,o0o or
more or affect the rental- value of his property by $1,200 per year
or more unless (1) all the properties that are roughly the saroe
distance away from the proposed project are similarly affected and
the property owners constitute a significant segnment of the
population, or (2) the effect on the remainder of the population
will be substantj.ally the same as the effect on him.
